data.title: Brady tightens adjusted EPS guidance to $4.48-$4.63 amid tariff exposure and ongoing cost actions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brady Corporation (BRC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • President and Chief Executive Officer Russell Shaller reported "another quarter of record high adjusted earnings per share," highlighting organic sales growth of 1.6%, acquisition-driven sales growth of 10.5%, and adjusted EPS growth of 11.9% for the quarter. Shaller emphasized "excellent organic sales growth of 5.4% and adjusted operating income growth of 20.2%" in the Americas and Asia region, while noting a "tough macro environment" in Europe and Australia with a 5.4% organic sales decline but a 3.8% increase in adjusted operating income due to restructuring.
  • Shaller announced the acquisition of Funai's Microfluidics Solutions business, stating it "further round[s] out our portfolio to enable custom part marking." He described continued integration of R&D and new product roadmaps, including direct part marking technologies.
  • Addressing tariffs, Shaller described the company’s largely in-country manufacturing strategy as mitigating tariff impact, but acknowledged "incremental tariffs and expect to continue to be impacted."
  • Chief Financial Officer Ann Thornton reported, "we grew adjusted diluted earnings per share from $1.09 per share last Q3 to a new record high of $1.22 per share, which is an increase of 11.9%." She highlighted restructuring actions and cost reductions, with "facility closure and other reorganization costs of $3.9 million in the third quarter."

Outlook

  • Thornton provided updated guidance, tightening the adjusted diluted EPS range from $4.45 to $4.70 per share to a new range of $4.48 to $4.63 per share for fiscal 2025. The company anticipates "organic sales growth in the low-single-digit percentages for the year ending July 31st, 2025," with depreciation and amortization expense of approximately $40 million, capital expenditures of approximately $25 million, and a full year income tax rate of approximately 20%.
  • Thornton stated, "Potential risks to our guidance, among others, include strengthening of the US dollar, inflationary pressures, continued changes in tariffs or other downstream impacts of global trade disruption or an overall slowdown in economic activity."

Financial Results

  • Thornton reported "organic sales were led by growth of 5.4% in the Americas and Asia region, which is partially offset by an organic sales decline of 5.4% in Europe and Australia, resulting in total organic sales growth of 1.6%."
  • Acquisitions contributed 10.5% to total sales growth, while foreign currency translation reduced sales by 0.7% for total sales growth of 11.4% in the quarter.
  • Gross profit margin was 51% compared to 51.6% in the prior year quarter, with $1.1 million in restructuring-related COGS. SG&A was $108.7 million, with an adjusted percentage of sales of 26.4%.
  • R&D expense increased 8.5% to $19.2 million, and operating cash flow was $59.9 million with free cash flow at $55.6 million. The company held a net cash position of $49.3 million as of April 30th and returned $44.5 million to shareholders.
  • Thornton noted "approximately $3 million in incremental tariff expense in the third quarter or about $0.05 of diluted earnings per share," with expected Q4 impact estimated at $3 million to $5 million, net of mitigating actions.

Q&A

  • Keith Housum, Northcoast Research Partners, asked about tariff impacts on demand and revenue. Shaller responded, "No, we haven't seen any tariff-related demand destruction at this point... you haven't really seen the effect of tariffs just yet."
  • Housum inquired about SG&A sustainability. Shaller answered, "our goal year-over-year is to continue to drive out costs... the goal is absolutely continue understanding that there will be a little bit of noise on a quarter-to-quarter."
  • On the Funai acquisition, Housum asked about strategic fit. Shaller explained, "We see this as a huge opportunity for a growth vector for Brady to round out, along with lasers, our ability to do direct part marking."
  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti, sought clarity on regional Q4 guidance. Shaller indicated headwinds from tariffs in the Americas and hoped for Europe to "get back to flat."
  • Ferazani inquired about China. Shaller described China as "a tough market and has become more tough," noting the closure of the Beijing facility and that "all of our China business is indigenous to China."
  • Ferazani asked about reorg costs and acquisition revenue impact. Thornton clarified, "The reorg costs were split basically pretty much evenly between the two regions... the acquisition is relatively small... we're estimating first year sales within the realm of $15 million to $20 million."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on tariff effects, cost controls, regional growth, and acquisition integration, with a slightly cautious and probing tone, particularly regarding tariff uncertainties and sustainability of cost reductions.
  • Management maintained an optimistic and confident tone, especially in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational resilience, strategic acquisitions, and ongoing cost management. During Q&A, management acknowledged uncertainties but reiterated mitigation strategies, with language such as "we hope" and "we're hoping" reflecting some caution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone remains cautious with continued focus on external risks, while management displays a similar level of confidence, though references to tariffs and macro uncertainty are more prominent.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was tightened to a narrower adjusted EPS range, compared to an increase of the low end in the previous quarter. Organic sales growth slowed to 1.6% from 2.6% in Q2, while adjusted EPS growth accelerated to 11.9% from 7.5%.
  • Strategic focus in Q3 included the acquisition of Funai's Microfluidics Solutions and continued investment in R&D, compared to Q2’s focus on the i7500 printer launch and Gravotech integration.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on tariffs, regional performance, and acquisitions, with persistent concerns around Europe, China, and cost controls.
  • Management’s confidence in cost containment and product launches remained high, but discussion of tariff risk and regional headwinds was more pronounced in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited incremental tariff exposure as a significant risk, with expected impacts of $3 million to $5 million in Q4. Mitigation actions include targeted price increases, strategic sourcing alternatives, and supply chain analysis.
  • Regional economic conditions, especially in Europe and China, remain challenging. Shaller stated, "the epicenter of the problem really is Germany with some collateral damage to some of the other industrialized countries in Europe."
  • Analyst concerns centered on the sustainability of cost savings and the effect of tariffs on demand and profitability.

Final Takeaway

Brady Corporation delivered another record adjusted EPS quarter, driven by strong growth in the Americas and Asia, ongoing R&D investment, and strategic acquisitions such as Funai's Microfluidics Solutions. Despite persistent macroeconomic and tariff uncertainties, management emphasized operational efficiency, disciplined capital allocation, and resilience through geographic and product diversification. The company tightened its adjusted EPS guidance and remains focused on mitigating external risks while investing in innovation and profitable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: South Bow reaffirms $1.01B EBITDA guidance for 2025 while addressing pipeline incident impacts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: South Bow Corporation (SOBO) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Bevin Wirzba, President and Chief Executive Officer, stated that "we had a solid financial start to 2025 and accomplished important milestones on our road to independence." Wirzba highlighted normalized EBITDA of $266 million for the quarter, successful completion of the pipeline scope of the Blackrod Connection Project, and implementation of a new enterprise resource planning system. He emphasized the company's commitment to returning value to shareholders, announcing a quarterly dividend of $0.50 per share payable on July 15th.
  • Wirzba discussed the Milepost 171 pipeline incident in North Dakota, stating, "maintaining safe and reliable operations was the most important goal we had to meet in becoming an independent company...no incident is ever acceptable at South Bow, but I'm proud of the way our team came together and demonstrated South Bow's ability to execute and get our system back up quickly and effectively."
  • The CEO reiterated the company's strong financial protections, noting that "90% of our EBITDA is contracted over the next seven years, meaning, there is no commodity price or volumetric risk in our base business." He reaffirmed normalized EBITDA guidance of $1.01 billion for 2025.
  • Richard Prior, Senior Vice-President and Chief Operating Officer, outlined the company's quick response to the pipeline incident, reporting, "our engineering and construction crews replaced the damaged section of pipe" and "we have completed removal of all contaminated soil from the site and we forecast to reclaim the site with clean soil in the coming weeks."
  • Van Dafoe, Senior Vice-President and Chief Financial Officer, stated, "South Bow's business is underpinned by highly contracted cash flows that are stable and predictable. Approximately 90% of our normalized EBITDA is contracted for over seven years." Dafoe reaffirmed the 2025 normalized EBITDA outlook of $1.01 billion and provided an updated range, adjusting the upside case to 1% from 3% and the downside case from a decrease of 3% to a decrease of 2% of the $1.01 billion base case.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed normalized EBITDA guidance of $1.01 billion for 2025. The upside case was narrowed to 1% from 3% of the base case, while the downside case improved to a decrease of 2% from a decrease of 3%.
  • Dafoe indicated, "we expect to exit 2025 with a net debt to normalized EBITDA ratio of approximately 4.8 times, increasing modestly through the year as we advance our Blackrod Connection Project and complete spin-off activities."
  • The outlook remains unchanged despite the pipeline incident, backed by stable contracted revenues and insurance coverage.

Financial Results

  • Wirzba reported normalized EBITDA of $266 million for the first quarter of 2025. The company declared a quarterly dividend of $0.50 per share.
  • Management noted that 90% of normalized EBITDA is contracted, supporting ongoing financial stability.
  • Insurance policies are expected to largely cover the costs associated with the pipeline incident, limiting financial exposure.

Q&A

  • Jeremy Tonet, JPMorgan Securities: Asked about the range of outcomes post-incident and potential impacts. Wirzba responded that the outlook is "fully underpinned" by contracted EBITDA and highlighted ongoing work with regulators and adjustments to pipeline maintenance plans as new information arises.
  • Tonet inquired about learnings from the incident. Wirzba emphasized ongoing investments in pipeline integrity and additional inline testing.
  • Robert Hope, Scotiabank: Questioned the duration of pressure restrictions and implications for spot volumes. Richard Prior stated it is "too early to speculate" on duration, with investigations expected to conclude by late summer, and explained that restrictions may be lifted in phases. Hope queried maintenance capital, to which Wirzba responded that planned work may shift between years depending on investigation outcomes, but operational costs are accounted for in variable tolls.
  • Maurice Choy, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about leverage and insurance recoveries. Dafoe replied, "we expect all costs related to Milepost 171 to be covered by insurance or through our variable toll."
  • Choy also questioned the outlook for spot volumes. Wirzba explained that additional headwinds, including tariffs and operational restrictions, have been layered onto the existing challenge of excess pipeline egress capacity, but the contracted business remains stable.
  • Robert Catellier, CIBC Capital Markets: Asked about the long-term 2%-3% EBITDA growth target and policy environment for projects. Wirzba referenced the Blackrod project as a contributor to 2026 growth and described a constructive permitting environment.
  • Benjamin Pham, BMO: Inquired about asset sales and First Nations partnerships. Wirzba stated that all assets are core but management would consider partnerships on new projects. Questions about the impact of industry consolidation were met with confidence in supply fundamentals supporting business strength.
  • Patrick Kenny, MBS: Asked about business development and inbound customer interest. Wirzba noted "pretty consistent interest" on both ends of the system and ongoing growth opportunities, with the company focused on strengthening its corridor.
  • Keith Stanley, Wolfe Research: Requested clarification on contractual commitments and insurance. Prior confirmed the company can physically move 585,000 barrels per day through the affected segment. Discussion of insurance coverage for potential future pipe replacement indicated such costs would generally be managed through variable tolls rather than insurance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised several questions on the financial and operational impact of the pipeline incident, pressure restrictions, and long-term growth prospects, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious tone as they sought clarity on risk mitigation, insurance coverage, and volumes. Questions focused on the details of incident costs, leverage implications, and the durability of guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone throughout, repeatedly emphasizing the stability of contracted cash flows, the effectiveness of insurance and contract structures, and the company's ability to meet guidance. Wirzba used phrases such as "no further risk on the base business" and highlighted prior investments in system integrity. When pressed, management responded directly, without visible defensiveness or hesitation.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a similar level of focus on risk management, deleveraging, and guidance stability, but the current quarter included more detailed exchanges around the operational incident and its financial implications.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter is marked by the operational incident at Milepost 171, with significant discussion around incident response, insurance coverage, and maintaining guidance. The previous quarter focused more on capital allocation, deleveraging, and growth opportunities, with less emphasis on operational disruptions.
  • Management reaffirmed guidance in both quarters, but in Q1 2025, the outlook range was tightened and the downside scenario was improved. The Blackrod Connection Project remains a central growth driver in both periods.
  • Analyst questions in the current quarter were more concentrated on incident-related risks, insurance recoveries, and operational constraints, while prior questions focused more on marketing headwinds, tariff exposure, and growth capital allocation.
  • Management’s tone remained confident in both quarters, with a continued emphasis on contracted revenue stability and prudent capital allocation.

Risks and Concerns

  • The key challenge discussed was the pipeline incident at Milepost 171, with pressure restrictions and ongoing investigations into the root cause. Management outlined mitigation through insurance, contracted revenues, and remedial maintenance activities.
  • The company acknowledged continued operational and market headwinds affecting uncommitted volumes, including added pressure from tariffs and excess pipeline egress capacity.
  • Analysts raised concerns about leverage, potential costs of the incident, and the impact of restrictions on future spot volumes, all addressed with assurances of insurance coverage and contract stability.

Final Takeaway

South Bow emphasized the resilience of its highly contracted business model, reaffirming its $1.01 billion normalized EBITDA guidance for 2025 despite the operational setback at Milepost 171. The company maintained its dividend, highlighted effective incident response, and communicated that insurance coverage and contract structures would mitigate the financial impact. Management signaled continued focus on safe operations, capital discipline, and growth through projects like Blackrod, while remaining transparent with stakeholders as the incident investigation progresses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Windtree Therapeutics announces resale of 42.17M shares by selling stockholders
data.description:
• Windtree Therapeutics (NASDAQ:WINT) is registering up to 42.17M shares of common stock for resale by certain selling stockholders.
  • These shares are issuable upon the conversion of Series D convertible preferred stock acquired under a securities purchase agreement dated April 29, 2025.
  • Windtree will not receive any proceeds from the resale of these shares by the selling stockholders.
  • WINT shares down 1% post-market on Friday.
  • Source: SEC filing

data.title: SA analyst downgrades: TSLA, T, PLTR, SMCI, ABR
data.description:
Here are the latest downgrades by Seeking Alpha analysts:

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA). Hold to sell by Oakoff Investments. "Long-term bets on robotaxis and humanoids are risky and won't offset near-term auto and energy business headwinds or margin compression. My valuation implies TSLA is overvalued by over 44%, and fundamentals don't justify current price action despite short-term momentum."

AT&T (NYSE:T). Buy to hold by Bay Area Ideas. "Despite solid fundamentals, technical indicators have turned bearish, with broken trend lines and negative momentum signals…Given the deteriorating technical outlook, I am downgrading AT&T to a Hold, encouraging investors to refrain from buying more for now."

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR). Buy to hold by Michael Del Monte. "Despite impressive growth, Palantir trades at a significant valuation premium, introducing heightened risk if growth slows or competition intensifies. Given the rich premium, I am downgrading Palantir to Hold with a $121.80 price target, reflecting a balanced risk-reward outlook at current levels."

Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI). Buy to sell by The Techie. "I recommend taking profits on Super Micro Computer stock after a 40% run, as the recent Saudi DataVolt deal is already priced in…FY26 guidance of $40 billion is overly optimistic and likely to be cut, with management lacking confidence and visibility."

Arbor Realty (NYSE:ABR). Buy to hold by Envision Research. 

Fortive (NYSE:FTV). Buy to hold by Badsha Chowdhury. 

On (NYSE:ONON). Buy to hold by LDV Research.

data.title: Weave Communications announces mixed shelf offering
data.description:
• Weave Communications (NYSE:WEAV) may offer and sell common stock, preferred stock, debt securities, warrants, rights, or units from time to time.
  • The debt securities could include debentures, notes, or other debt instruments.
  • Preferred stock, debt, warrants, and rights may be convertible or exchangeable into other securities, including Weave’s common or preferred stock.
  • Weave will not receive any proceeds from the sales.
  • Source: SEC filing

data.title: CoreWeave soars as investors pile in after Q1 results
data.description:
• CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) shares soared more than 22% on Friday, as investors appeared to reassess the cloud service provider's first-quarter results that received high praise on Wall Street.
  • Shares have doubled since the company held its initial public offering in March, raising $1.5B in its offering.
  • Several sell-side firms, including Wells Fargo, Morgan Stanley and Bank of America, raised their price targets on CoreWeave, with one analyst stating it has become a “core holding” for investors.
  • The company also disclosed in its 10-Q filing that OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, will pay it up to $4B through April 2029 to provide cloud computing capacity.
  • For the period ending March 31, CoreWeave said revenue rose 420.3% year-over-year to $981.6M. The company ended the period with a revenue backlog of $25.9B, including remaining performance obligations of $14.7B.

(This story has been updated to include additional information and closing price.)

data.title: Biofrontera signals 2025 record revenue ambitions with new Ameluz patent and cost controls
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Biofrontera Inc. (BFRI) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Hermann Luebbert opened with, "Our first quarter was a busy and exciting period for us. We continued our revenue growth while keeping our costs under control. Total revenues for the first quarter of 2025 were $8.6 million, a 9% increase from the same period of the prior year." He emphasized, "We strongly believe our past investments, execution and tremendous efforts to increase the effectiveness of our sales force will allow us to achieve record revenues in 2025 without increasing our costs."
  • Luebbert highlighted the granting of a new patent on the reformulated Ameluz, noting, "This new formulation, which lacks the potential allergen propylene glycol, has already been approved by the FDA and is in use since last year. Having a patent protection on this Ameluz formulation until December 2043, gives us another 18.5 years of protection from generic competition."
  • He described clinical development milestones, including the enrollment of the final patient in a Phase III trial for Ameluz in actinic keratoses on the extremities, neck, and trunk. The company is also pursuing FDA approval for label extension and for Ameluz in superficial basal cell carcinoma and acne.
  • Luebbert stated, “We expect to submit the new data to the FDA in the second half of this year. Following the approvals for AK on the entire body and for superficial basal cell carcinoma, we are aiming to -- at getting Ameluz approved for the treatment of moderate to severe acne.”
  • CFO Fred Leffler reported, "Total revenues for the first three months ended March 31, 2025, were $8.6 million, an increase of $0.7 million or 8.7% as compared to the three months ended March 31, 2024." Leffler detailed, "Cost of revenues, related party were $3.1 million for the first quarter of 2025 compared with $4.0 million for the prior year quarter. This decrease of $0.9 million or 22.1% compared to last year was due to the reduced cost structure under the last amendment of the Ameluz license and supply agreements."
  • Leffler highlighted, "The net loss for the first quarter of 2025 was $4.2 million or $0.47 per share, compared with a net loss of $10.4 million or $2.88 per share for the prior year quarter."

Outlook

  • Management expects to achieve record revenues in 2025, supported by increased sales force effectiveness and cost controls. Luebbert explained, “We believe we have built the foundation with the sales team and back end support to continue to improve our results on a consistent basis.”
  • The company plans to submit new data on Ameluz for label expansion to the FDA in the second half of 2025 and expects Phase II acne study data toward the end of the year.
  • No quantitative guidance or numerical projections for the coming quarters were disclosed in the transcript.

Financial Results

  • Revenues for Q1 2025 were $8.6 million, up $0.7 million year-over-year, primarily attributed to increases in Ameluz sales and the launch of the RhodoLED XL lamp.
  • Operating expenses fell to $13.1 million from $13.4 million the prior year quarter, with cost of revenues, related party down to $3.1 million from $4.0 million.
  • SG&A expenses decreased by $0.6 million, while research and development expenses increased by $1.2 million due to the assumption of all clinical trial activities for Ameluz in the US.
  • Net loss narrowed to $4.2 million, with improved gross profit and adjusted EBITDA, driven by cost reductions and sales growth.
  • Cash and cash equivalents were $1.8 million as of March 31, 2025, down from $5.9 million at the end of 2024.

Q&A

  • Jonathan Aschoff, ROTH Capital: "Over 1Q '25, how many lamp units did you sell, both the original and the XL?" Fred Leffler: "Placements as of Q1 were 18 -- we placed 18 of the XL lamps." Aschoff followed up about original lamp sales, to which Leffler replied he would need to double check the number.
  • Aschoff asked about sales force attrition and cost savings. Leffler responded, "We're working on bringing in some more -- what we call like a more junior rep... Some of that comes with a bit lower salary. And then some of it is -- has been some turnover, but we're committed to replacing that and reorganizing the territories and the team to be as efficient as possible to finish out the year strong."
  • Bruce Jackson, The Benchmark Company: Asked about the impact of transfer pricing changes on gross margins for the rest of the year. Leffler replied, "All of the inventory we have now is at the 25% transfer price, and that's what we will see for the rest of the year. It might be offset...based on how many lamps we sell because the margin there is lower."
  • Jackson also inquired about reimbursement status for the three tube indication. Luebbert said, "We are not aware of a single case where a doctor has been refused payment because of using more than one tube. So this seems to be completely solved."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral, focused on clarification of lamp sales, sales force structure, gross margin impacts, and reimbursement. Questions were direct without overt skepticism or enthusiasm.
  • Management maintained a confident and slightly positive tone, emphasizing strategic milestones, cost controls, and clinical progress. Luebbert highlighted, “We believe we have built the foundation...to continue to improve our results on a consistent basis.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained stable, with management reiterating confidence in achieving record results, and analysts maintaining a fact-finding approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2025 revenue growth (9% year-over-year) was highlighted, following a record sales year in 2024.
  • Cost controls continued to be a focus, with reductions in cost of revenues and SG&A, while R&D expenses rose due to clinical trial activities.
  • Strategic initiatives remained consistent, with the new Ameluz patent and clinical trial milestones representing the main developments this quarter.
  • Management's confidence remained strong, with a continued emphasis on reaching breakeven and expanding Ameluz indications. Analysts' tone was unchanged, focusing on operational details and growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted increased legal expenses related to patent claims, partially offset by savings elsewhere.
  • Cash and cash equivalents declined to $1.8 million, which may indicate a need for further capital or careful cash management.
  • The company is dependent on successful clinical trial outcomes and FDA approvals for key growth initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Biofrontera management underscored a quarter of progress in revenue growth, cost discipline, and product innovation, anchored by a new long-term patent for Ameluz and advancing clinical milestones. With an explicit expectation of record revenue for 2025 and ongoing label expansion efforts, the company continues to prioritize efficiency and market penetration while navigating legal and cash flow challenges. Investors are guided to watch for upcoming FDA submissions and data readouts as pivotal drivers for the remainder of the year.
